I purchased the original Cities Skylines in December 2021 and fell in love with the game. When a sequel was announced with all the new features I was hyped for, but when I started playing I realised the lack of features I had received from purchasing the majority of the CS:1 DLCs so I abandoned and continued playing the original.  A few years go by and I revisited the game last year with mods available, added some mods, made the game better and noticed the poor optimisation. Over the past year, and especially in the last few months since Iceflake Studios has become the developer, the game runs so much better than previously. With the addition of configuring some settings on my PC, depending on the size of my city I can easily get 40-60 fps on 4k graphics.  Overall, poor, rushed, and early release by the game is finally living up to the hype. The game is easily now in a state to properly succeed the original.

A lot of people are talking about how bad this game is and how terribly optimized it is, but as someone with almost 45 hours at the time of this review, i feel like this is partially unwarranted. I'm going to give you an in depth explanation of the game, the ups and the downs, and everything else you need to know to make an informed decision about this game.   First lets address the negative reviews. I personally have had no experience with any crashes in all my time playing CS2, so I cannot properly respond to them. However I will put my own theories out there. Theory one is that your playing on high graphic settings, which puts immense strain on your GPU, which I will return to later. Theory two is that you either have super massive 1m+ population cities, or you have mods that make citizen AI more advanced. The next complaint is how taxing CS2 is on your GPU. Any game with high enough graphics settings will make your GPU heat up.  The game itself is definitely in need of lots more work, that I don't deny. However though, to say it is terrible due to citizen AI being broken, and a lot of these other claims isn't fair to the game, because for a casual player these problems have never came up for myself. I do play with mods, but even on its own, vanilla CS2 is plenty fun, and I have a whole city going and its up to half a million citizens.  CS2 is properly paced, contrary to what some people say about it. It doesn't take too long to get from a small town to having high density residential. Each resident has their own life, leading to even better progression because you not only need to handle expanding the city, but also rush hour, and just plain residents going to the store, or the park. The game certainly has better progression then CS1, and allows you to choose what service you want to upgrade. This creates really interesting cities, and no two games are every quite the same.  The main problems the game has is that it over promised on release, and still hasn't quite lived up to expectations.  On top of that the publisher, Paradox Interactive, are incredibly greedy, and more often then not, updates adding new features come out as DLCs. The game itself is hilariously overpriced, and as Dr Seuss put it, I wouldn't touch it with a ten foot pool. However, when on sale I would definitely recommend it. Personally I think a price tag of 29.99$ is much more suitable for the game, rather then the fifty were currently paying.  In the end I definitely say that Cities: Skylines 2 is worth getting, even in the state it is in, just wait until it gets on sale. However the purpose of this is not to tell you to buy it or not to buy it, like other reviews, but rather to help you to make a informed decision.

As a longtime Cities: Skylines player, I finally picked up Cities: Skylines II last week expecting a complete disaster based on what I'd read online. Instead, I found myself doing exactly what a city builder is supposed to make me do: losing track of time while obsessing over traffic, zoning, and infrastructure. Is the game perfect? Not even close. But somewhere between the criticism and the memes, people forgot that it's actually fun. I'd rather play an ambitious city builder with flaws than a polished one that plays it safe. The game still has work ahead of it, but if the biggest problem is that I sat down for an hour and accidentally played for six, I think it's doing something right.

I read many comments and none really answered the question. So in all, CS2 is better as it is more like you're actually building a city and you can make a whole city with the towns that's very realistic not just from graphic, but from gameplay. Now, they're definitely are some flaws that are better in CS1, like highways have signs, you can remove zones, etc. but CS1 is more of a game that's more unrealistic in how a city functions. And of course, it will have a lower quality in gameplay and graphic as it was made in 2015. It's also true, that a lot of mods can definitely enhance and "carry" CS2, but quit frankly for me the mods haven't buffed the experience as I haven't used them, but they can definitely knock CS1 out of the ball park when used and CS2 have mods built into the game.

The largest improvements over the original are: improved road construction tools, upgradable-expandable buildings, more realistic simulation, better graphics.     The performance has been improved a lot but it will still require a powerful PC with a hefty CPU and GPU. Mine can handle about 330k citizens before it slows down dramatically, which is ok because in this game that equates to a very substantially sized city.     Installling mods is easy but they are much more glitchy than in CS1, there are far fewer expansions and not as many things to do in this game but you can spend more time on watching and refining your cities growth, solving problems that feel more realistic rather than just pure volume of various buildings.     I hoped the graphics would have been even better for all the money one needs to put into a PC to run the game well which is a big shame. The other big dissapointment is that there is still quite a lot of infrastructure that you can build but it is not clear how it directly affects your city. For example, if you build custom park areas with bike paths or leave more green space between residential zones, it doesn't seem to improve land value or make the buildings upgrade, improve happiness. It will make for better commutes although this is hard to see in actuality. It would be nice to see every layount and construction you make have a tangible impact.     I still recommend the game but if your PC is older, you can be very happy with SC1 and all of its amazing expansions.

City Skylines 2 is a successful and major upgrade in the franchise. It's greatest downfall was it's terrible performance but under it's new team even that has now turned around. I have personally have had no performance issues with the game. When it comes to the actual gameplay, I personally find that City Skylines 2 is superior to first game in almost every way. It looks incredible, the assets are better designed and more seamless, the progression system is more enjoyable, and I find it has far more to offer in terms of gameplay. If you were a fan of the old game, or if you are new the franchise, I would highly recommend   City Skylines 2. Un-popular opinion but I am not sure how people find the first game even holds a candle to this sequel apart from performance, which to be fair was horrid upon release but seems perfectly fine now. I think, when running properly, this game is just the better game all around.

Cities Skylines 2 was promised to be a huge step forward for the community when the trailer came out, but since those days the game has been nothing short of an embarrassment. Way to complexly modeled cims, poorly optimized gameplay, missing content from the first game, and so many more valid complaints led to a huge change from Paradox to dismiss Colossal Order from development.  Having Iceflake take over this franchise was a huge shift in the right direction, the game runs so much better (even though it's not hitting the benchmarks it still needs to get to), the team is actually responsive and engaging with the community (e.g. we had a loading screen bug and it took them no time at all to release a hotfix and patch it), and there's finally a sign of life within the game again.  If you're on the fence about this game, I'd genuinely consider getting it. To me, it's finally beginning to live up to the standards of a Cities game. There's still some work to be done, but I'm glad about my purchase and I have very high hopes. I've logged quite some time this week and have had a blast.

⭐ Rating: 6/10 ⭐  Positives: ✔ Improved graphics compared to Cities: Skylines I—cities look much more realistic and alive. ✔ Better road options with more flexibility and control for builders. ✔ UI is much better now—cleaner and easier to manage compared to the first game. ✔ Addictive as always—you can easily lose hours building your city. ✔ Micromanagement is one of the best parts of the game and better than many other city-builders.  Negatives: ❌ Separate mods system (not using Steam Workshop) is prone to mod errors and compatibility issues. ❌ Heavy game in both graphics usage and file size—needs strong hardware. ❌ Takes quite a while to load into the game. ❌ Bugs, glitches, and sudden crashes still happen too often.  Overall, Cities: Skylines II improves many core systems from the first game and offers deeper city-building mechanics, but performance problems and technical issues hold it back. Still very fun and addictive for city-builder fans. 🏙️🚦🎮

Honestly, Cities: Skylines 2 is everything I wanted from a sequel and more. The level of detail in the simulation is insane—your citizens actually feel alive, and every decision you make has a real impact on how your city grows.  The road-building tools are super smooth and way more intuitive than before, which makes designing complex infrastructure actually fun instead of frustrating. I also love how much freedom you get—you can build your city exactly how you imagine it, whether you're going for efficiency or pure creativity.    Visually, the game looks great, and watching your small town slowly turn into a huge, busy metropolis is incredibly satisfying. It’s the kind of game where you say “just 10 more minutes” and suddenly it’s 3 AM.  Overall, a deep, addictive city builder with tons of potential. Definitely worth it if you enjoy simulation and management games.  Recommended 👍

They've improved the game a ton since launch even though they took their sweet time to release the first patch(it was like a year and a half after the buggy launch). It seems to be updated at a regular rate now and much more stable in the current patch too. I enjoy that the DLC is mostly cosmetic and all gameplay features are included in the base game, as opposed to the first game. I have 40 or 50 more hours than Steam shows since I had the game on gamepass before they decided to triple the price.  The performance could be better but it does look pretty good and my CPU is outdated. Gameplay wise it is improved a lot. A bigger map that can be unlocked fully with free landscaping that isn't slow as hell like it was in the first, it works as well as it did in the map editor in the regular game now. Improved resource simulation where you can create materials that businesses use in the city to avoid them having to import them, allowing for increased profits for that business and the one creating those material both giving you more sweet taxes. This system straight up didn't work in the release version but it seems to now and it's great. You can build powerlines underground and they work like pipes now, so you never have to waste space for those ever again. You can change the road signs and lights and stuff, so if you want to remove crosswalks and street lights at an intersection to speed up traffic at risk of accidents you could totally do that if you forget that roundabouts exist lol. The only thing I wish was in this game that's in the first is you can't spawn natural disasters yourself, although they are a part of the base game rather than entirely DLC locked.

Cities: Skylines 2 is game with a troubled past, but I'm optimistic about it's future. It's probably never going to be the most perfomant game, but if you lower your settings, you can get this to run better than you think these days. I run the game at 1280 x 1024 on my second monitor, getting about 30fps on medium settings in a city of about 10k and with most of the official asset packs from paradox mods. This is on an AMD RX 580. I have a higher end machine but it currently runs Linux and I've not yet tried it on there.  In my opinion the base game mechanics are more fun to interact with than their predecessor even with several mechanics from that game not yet implemented. In CS1 I found that in order to build the city I wanted I needed to use mods to basically ignore the game mechanics and turn it into a sandbox. CS2 allows me to build a beautiful city while still playing a fun management game. Game is worth it if you're the type to spend thousands of hours building the perfect city.

The game is fine and I truly believe it still has massive potential. I’ve been playing on and off, but I find myself with way too many maps and saves since the game requires such a high level of planning.   Performance-wise, I’m averaging about 28 FPS, which is perfectly fine for me. I use a custom settings profile—a sweet spot between Medium and High—to balance visual quality with my hardware limitations. While there are definitely hiccups, spikes, and glitches, especially once the population hits the 100k mark, I still have high hopes for the new dev team.   I’m firmly "Team CS2" over the original because I prefer the aesthetics and the increased node capacity. It’s hard to please everyone, but it’s a solid, "OK" game right now—not amazing, but certainly not bad. I just hope the developers continue to optimize it further and eventually integrate more QoL mods and legacy features from its predecessor into the base game.

Big Cities Skylines fan here. Loved the first game so wanted to leave me two cents on this one. I should start by stating this i my first time playing on PC so I am unsure how the other game worked on PC. There are a lot of negative comments for this game and it is understandable. Here's what I think:    I think the game itself is very enjoyable if you want to get home from work, decompress, and play a chill game. I do think it has a long way to go in terms of graphics and looking more seamless. However, the MODs definitely help out. I watched a video on Youtube by City Planner Plays which really helped (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PG6J6EWBfJQ) figure out what is useful, what the game is missing which can be added through MODs, and what can even help with bugs and framerate. Kudos to this man!    Overall, descent game, and from the looks of things, the game is being worked on by Paradox to correct what the other company had messed up. There seems to be a lot of future updates which could make the game much more enjoyable. The only thing I am not a fan of is the city progression. You unlock roads and other in-game pieces through XP now instead of the original population. I feel this is limiting. The first game allowed you to reach a population and unlock a lot of different things to use in game. Now you have to unlock said items after reaching XP levels. Minor cosmetic which can be learned and I guess okay later. The big thing I don't care for is the graphics. I have a solid PC that I bought and it seems to be incapable of making the game look "fluid". When you are zoomed it the game looks nice, but as you zoom out buildings and roads can look grainy and unfocused. Maybe its my monitor, maybe its how the game is, and maybe its something to get used to. Hopefully through updates, this will change to allow my PC to show its raw power and show my city more detailed from above.    Other than that don't fall into the trap of negative comments. The game runs well, the game is fun, and just has a few differences. The MODs that people place in the MOD store make the game a lot of fun and is way more interactive than its counterpart. I do think the price is a little steep with it not being "complete" but worth it in my opinion!

Cities: Skylines II is exactly what a sequel should be. It takes everything that made the first game great and refines it into a deeper, more advanced city-building experience.    The simulation is on another level. Traffic flows more naturally, citizens behave in believable ways, and the economy feels interconnected and responsive. Every decision has weight, which makes planning and managing your city far more engaging and rewarding.    The scale is impressive. You’re not just building a city—you’re shaping a living system that evolves over time. From small towns to massive urban centers, the progression feels smooth and satisfying.    Visually, the game is a big step up. The detail, lighting, and overall atmosphere make cities feel alive, especially as they grow and infrastructure starts working in harmony.    Performance is solid, and the experience is smooth, allowing you to focus entirely on building and optimizing your city without distractions. I know that people have given this game a lot of grief because it wasn't exactly like it's predecessor upon launch and I sympathize with that.  I spent hours and hours in CS1 getting things laid out and modded so that the towns and cities I created looked exactly how I wanted them to.      CS2 has had its ups and downs.  I love the UI and a lot of the tweaks that they did from the drop.  It was disappointing to have paid so much and not have the same sorts of things I did with CS1.  But I get it.  It was basically a rewrite of the whole game.  That takes a lot of time and effort to do when you're not just slapping lipstick on a pig and calling it good.  People sometimes forget how bad SimCity 5 ended up being despite being the best version of SimCity made.  They put so much effort into that but then made it of the cloud; such a bad idea.  At least CS2 wasn't that.    It's nice to see this game progressing albeit slowly, it's still moving forward.  I would suggest this game to anyone who likes city builders.  It's a lot of fun, and you're able to do a lot of creative things to make your city come to life.  The packs and mods now add even more to it.      It was definitely worth the wait for the game to grow a little.

All things considered, I really don't like direction paradox is going with chapter/bundle dlc, as it either needs to be cheaper or have more content. I program and I understand, I'm not asking for much.     But CS2 is still the creme de le creme of city builders (and better than CS1 in current state imo, water update was huge). For $50 this game is still not worth it. Compared to the first there are a good amount of refinements, but the game itself runs quite poorly. I have a city with 250,000 people that can't run at the standard simulation speed. I still have like half of the map left to build on so it's not like I'm in the end game. I think that's my biggest gripe, the performance issues.   Outside of that there are issues with how the game functions fundamentally. Zoning is weird sometimes and hard to fix, the in-game menus for transit, healthcare, education, etc can be kind of janky and hard to work with. I wish there was more information available about how the simulation determines what your city needs, just more advanced statistics, ya know.  Is it better than CS1? Maybe. Comparing the base game of each, I'd say CS2 is probably better. With DLC CS1 has more features and content, but suffers from jank, roadbuilding in particular. CS2 barely has any DLC, and what does exist is mostly just assets, not actual gameplay additions. All of this being said, I've so far stuck with CS2 since buying it. Maybe I'll return to CS1 at some point though and re-evaluate.  Lastly I'll say that Iceflake studios seems to be putting their best foot forward. All they've done so far is small bug fixes and menu adjustments, which if that's all they end up doing then who cares. But if they eventually get to fixing the more fundamental issues then this game could eventually be the great game it was promised to be.

So i didnt plan for this review to be so long, but It ended up being quite long... oops. at first i might have given this game an OK review... but my game suddenly crashes (just leaves the game) so many times, at least once every 3 or so hours of playing. theres nothing wrong with my PC, its high powered, and I dont have any weird, old or bad mods, only the usual like Anarchy, the different housing themes, etc. Twice, since I've played it recently, I've started a new city, played for a couple of hours, then its crashed and I have lost ALL of my progress - hours of work. Maybe if it had happened once or twice over a longer period it would have been fine, but twice in less than three days? loosing ALL of my progress? is there no constant (every 10-30 mins) autosave in the game?  Additionally to continue on about the mods, particularly the different zoning housing themes, they dont fill all the area well, unlike the base ones? i understand the houses dont have all the variations (1 by 1 to 6 by 6). If you have any sugestions for that please tell me.  Lastly, the traffic system and housing numbers are so out of wack. It requires you to build such a large area of low density housing for only a small amount of facilites/other zoning. the Ai traffic is frankly terrible choosing the worst paths and not using freeways, and there are so many for just a small number of houses. I know a lot of this may technically be realistic because we need a lot of low dencity housing and have a large reliance of cars irl, but is it better to be realistic or better for the player, while still being slightly realistic. also, just wondering if you can fix the traffic with transport, like if you had more public transport, will the amount of traffic decrease?  (p.s. why is there so much foot traffic???) (p.s.s why are all the good things in the buyable mods (e.g. fishing, harbors, cool bridges, tourist stuff)???, can we get some more stuff in the base game please??? Can we normalise not having 1000 DLC's? like the new game paralives, i understand people have to get their bag, and they're not indi developers, but damn can we alease get some other types of zoned buildings other than europe and america(+there is only one type of low density commercial zones?!?!?!), more tourist stuff and more than one harbor/airport type/size pleaaaase???)  Other than that, the game has a lot of poteintal, not unlike the first one - they're very similar, I'd even say this one is a downgrade. the graphics are great, the road system are good, and the maps are great. my favorite things are the varity of the low density housing (+others), the ability to get mods in the game that download easily and not having to pay for terraforming (+ the terraforming system is very good). I hope with this new devs the game will improve, and i am hopeful.  Overall, I cant wait to see where this game goes, i wouldn't recomend people to get the game in its current state (unless its on a big sale - which is what I did), but in the future it should be a definite.

I am giving this a Not Recommend for now in the interest  that this game continues to improve. At launch, I refunded the game because of performance issues, but in its current state I believe that for casual players like me who just enjoy balancing supply chains and building a giant urban sprawl, this game is a good purchase on a sale. That being said, there are a couple annoying items that detract from the fun, most notably with managing zoning on specific tiles and not having a way to change out industry supply chains aside from demolishing over and over until you get the company (and product) you want. Overall if you're looking to upgrade from CS1 and aren't invested in modding, this is worth your time, but it still needs more improvements to be truly special. I am having fun. However, for every improvement and depth Cities Skyline 2 adds on top of Cities Skylines 1, it also seemingly removes control and buries information in abstract ways, removing agency from the player. This doesn't need to continue to be the case and I belive the current developers will make this game better.

I’m playing Cities: Skylines and honestly can’t understand why it’s so boring. Even SimCity 2000 Deluxe had more things to do and felt more alive as a city-builder. It genuinely feels like the developers don’t understand what makes this genre interesting.  Why has nobody tried combining the depth of Transport Fever with the city planning of Cities: Skylines? Seriously, if Transport Fever allowed zoning like Cities does, Cities: Skylines would die overnight.  It’s such a painfully mediocre city-builder.  The only franchise that still consistently delivers is Anno — and that’s despite Ubisoft being behind it.  Without mods, there’s basically no reason to even play Cities: Skylines. It’s just not interesting.  I’m genuinely baffled by how awful the industrial zoning system is. Who thought this was acceptable design? Why are there no interactions with businesses or investors? Why can’t entrepreneurs approach the player with offers like: “I want to build a farm on this area, and in return I’ll provide taxes, jobs, and investment”? Where’s the actual economic simulation or meaningful decision-making?  You’d have to be unbelievably uninspired to create such a primitive city-builder in the 2020s.  I honestly don’t understand what I paid so much money for.  I could’ve just downloaded SimCity 2000 and had a far more engaging experience.  Even indie games like Manor Lords and Memoriapolis have more interesting and thoughtful city-building systems. At least those games have identity and ambition.  Cities: Skylines just feels like soulless garbage.  I’m honestly in disbelief.  This is just an incredibly primitive city-builder. We’re not living in the early ’90s or 2000s anymore, where people would happily accept gameplay this shallow and repetitive. It’s a complete disappointment.  What makes it even worse is that instead of acknowledging the core problems and making a genuinely better game, they keep trying to rehabilitate and patch over a fundamentally weak design.  Let me clarify something: I’m not even judging the optimization here. I can live with 20 FPS — I have a mid-range laptop, I wasn’t expecting miracles. My problem with Cities: Skylines is purely about gameplay.  If your only goal is to build a pretty city and take screenshots, then maybe you’ll enjoy it. Although even for that, you’ll probably end up installing mods for proper assets, road tools, and decent customization.  But personally, I enjoy city-builders for completely different reasons. I love managing profits, citizen satisfaction, economic balance, and long-term planning. I want the game to give me soft objectives and reward me for solving problems creatively.  Things like: “Build a farm in this district and receive subsidies.” “Allow heavy industry near the reservoir for higher tax income, but risk polluting the city’s water supply.” “Support local businesses to increase employment.” They could’ve added corporations (not just transport companies) like in OpenTTD, competing with each other over cities, proposing initiatives, lobbying different communities, and so on. Like, one group wants ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ bike lanes, while these oil-loving dudes want more parking spaces for their absurdly huge semis and trucks.  So basically, where’s the conflict between environmentalists and car addicts, corporations and farmers? Where are the protests from unhappy citizens? This isn’t city-building — it’s just urban planning for a pretty screenshot.   That’s actual gameplay.  Those kinds of systems already existed in older strategy games like Zeus + Poseidon, Caesar, Pharaoh, and even that amazing ancient China city-builder I can’t remember the name of right now. Even back then, these games tried to simulate meaningful governance and city management.  Meanwhile, Cities: Skylines boils down to this:  Paint zones. Optimize traffic. Raise taxes. Congratulations: +$13,371,488,322 per second.  That’s it.  It feels incredibly shallow.  There’s barely any meaningful control over city development. Where are district-specific incentives? Where are subsidies? Where are policies designed for individual neighborhoods? Why are taxes mostly tied to the entire city instead of specialized districts and local economies?  Everything feels oversimplified to the point of absurdity.  And the funniest part is that the only decent assets are mostly locked behind DLCs — but honestly, even those aren’t worth it. You’re better off using mods because most of the official content feels just as mediocre.  So honestly: buy the game only if it’s massively discounted. Or pirate it first just to understand how empty it actually is.  I genuinely don’t understand what they spent all those years developing. Everywhere you look, it feels unfinished and primitive: poor optimization, shallow gameplay, barely any real simulation.  Sorry for the wall of text, but this game genuinely pissed me off. Especially because I bought this ♥♥♥♥ with all the DLCs.

I really want to enjoy the game. It scratches that itch I get with 'make it bigger' and all the trains/subways/etc I love  What makes me give this a bad review? It kinda just sucks to put it lightly   Many of my 'traffic jams' are created by people just... blocking the intersection, and many times I've watched painstakingly close seeing if I've missed something but no, they just create their own problems  Building roads is very annoying (and not the whole science and traffic stuff). You place some road and think- 'Oh wait, let me change it to this road type instead'. Well... you're going to have to delete the whole road, and try not to have them merge together and form un-needed intersections, or clip through the ground, or fidget with the columns of overpasses, and 'blah blah'  (Found this out after trying to edit a highway) If you have powerlines on a street you cannot edit that street what so ever- even if you want to make it a 'no left turn' or anything. I don't get why that is a problem but I guess why not  I have a mailbox on a street, that street also has a post office, there are some building there, they are complaining about the lack of postal service in their area. There is no more explanation I can give on that  For some reason people find it smart to get into the right lane (Exit ramp) of a highway, then within the short distance of that decide they need to merge back into the left lane- which creates traffic jams   There's so many tiny problems, some big problems, and otherwise 'problems'. There is mod options for the game which I do use to *sorta* fix certain things but... there's just so much wrong with it. If this was in beta/early access, I'd give them all passes on this, but it's not. It is very annoying not becuase the 'game is too hard' (I admit I am a bit stupid but that's what I love about it too) it's just... annoying   Would I pay full price for the game? Absolutely not and you shouldn't either- I don't' care if you're going into civil engineering or what ever  Would I pay... 20-30$ for this? Mmmmm... maybe  Do I even own any of the DLC? Nope. And really it feels that much more as a cash grab given the current state of the game while pumping out 10+ DLC which is either cosmetics or 'Things that should be in the base game'  It's not the worst game, and despite all the complaints I do recommend it. Just not for 50$, and 30$ is also pushing it for me   *Would* give a 8/10 if you 'get the game by cheaper means' but otherwise 3/10 or something I don't care

It is difficult to write about this game, for to play it one must dodge all bugs, losses of FPS, broken mechanics etc...  The first game was a gem, made better by all the mods. The game was poached and monetized, very sad. Here are a few points:  -The base game is buggy and demanding for a system (even on a good system: Processor	Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-14900HX, 2200 Mhz, 24 Core(s), 32 Logical Processor(s).) The game invariably ends up crashing at some point when the city starts developping. -The city has unrealistic demands as time advances. Rarely do we find an IRL city with more than a couple of universities, more than 10 hospitals, or a stupid amount of schools.  -The localisation of special buildings don't seem to attract people there. Commercial buildings don't attract people who need to buy stuff, schools don't attract students, monuments/touristic buildings don't attract tourists. They create zones that fulfill "needs" for housing or the such, but this makes the whole experience empty because we are not zoning a city for people but for a spreadsheet with a sense of directions.  -The movement of people with public transport, by foot or by car do not follow the logic of the zoning. Car jams will happen no matter what when you reach a certain density. People are just an image, an afterthought, they might as well not exist at all. They will create problems for the player to solve, but you cannot solve their movements, they are not ruled by logic. -Road management, not as precise or realistic as the first game. The cars will not obey the preselections and road priorities. Jams will happen no matter what you do. Crossings mean nothing to no one, they change nothing and are only visual.  -Pedestrians will do anything they want, walk anywhere, not use transport... Anything goes. -We get a lot of spreadsheet based games from paradox, and fortunately there are mods... But this time they have taken a game, stolen the ideas from the first game, given us the most basic version of the concept, started selling anything of importance for the game as a DLC. Shameless, soulless, criminal to the people who loved the first game.  -I believed this game would be a huge improvement, but now 3 years after it came out, we still have a husk of an unplayable game. At best it is a visual sandbox with a few graphs displaying numbers affected by other building's proximity. edit:- i forgot that the radio stations are a repetitive hell of lounge music interspersed by five unfunny jokes and references to dubious characters.  Huge disappointment, game will never be more than an expensive cashgrab, will never be improved on.  It might even spell the end of what could have been an incredible series.  I miss the first game, the community, the mods, even if DLC heavy - they had a meaning still.   Not recommended

Maybe with time I would recommend this game, but in its current state I can't.   It does improve on roads and frequently modded aspects of the original. However it takes a massive step back on having core functions included in the base game, and is a huge resource drain on your computer. It is prettier, but the functionality does not outweigh that. The changes to industry and terrain I don't like, instead of brushes you draw out an area that is very rigid. I got the game on a discount, but I feel more regret than excitement to play it.   The modding has changed drastically and it's very frustrating, removing the Steam community aspect and forcing you to stick to Paradox is a huge step backwards. You also have to pay for many functions that should've been included in the first place. Skylines has always been a bit of a cash grab, but this version is insane.   If you want a city builder, purchase the original or look elsewhere. I hope this changes with time, but three years in and there are still basic issues.

I have never written a review on a game before but this is annoying. I've tried really hard to like this game. Added mods, ect. But the one thing that is making me hate this game is the traffic. With the first game, I was able to fix the intersections to make traffic flow nicely. But with this, there are so many things that are making traffic worse.   Roundabouts: have pedestrian crossings but do not allow you to take them out or add lights. This makes it so traffic is always at a standstill because there are so many pedestrians. They refuse to take the bridge so not even making one is helpful.   Uturns: cars are now making unnecessary uturns right in the middle of the street which holds up traffic. No ability to add fines/make illegal.   They took out all of the city policies so that isn't an option to have city wide policies. Which I personally liked from the old game.   Had to add a mod in order to adjust the lights, which is fine, however it would be nice to be able to adjust the lights to be timed better to allow the busiest road to go for longer than the lanes sitting empty.   If they just focused on fixing the traffic, this would be an amazing game, better than the old one. But it still has a very long way to go.

I've been playing this game since launch, and it's always been a love-hate relationship. But over time, I've been enjoying it less and less. For me, a game doesn't need to be perfect, which is also the reason why I gave Cities: Skylines 2 so many chances. But at this point, I'm just frustrated by all the crashes and performance issues the game still has.  I can see the improvements over time, but it's already been more than two and a half years since launch. At the moment, I just don't see myself enjoying the game anymore whenever I start it. You always have to wait absurd amounts of time for all your mods to be updated, to load into your city, and for the game itself to start at first, of course. Usually, I'm a patient person, but my patience ends when I have to manually click through every error report and restart the game, only to repeat the whole process all over again because some mods are no longer compatible. I'm looking forward to the improvements Iceflake Studios will provide, but for now, I'll continue my long-lasting break, hoping that one day I'll be able to start the game and actually enjoy it again.  If you're currently deciding whether the price is worth it, you should either wait a couple more years or give the game a try without expecting great performance unless your PC is at least mid-range. I played it at 12 FPS all low settings for about six months until I upgraded my PC, and now it's always a great heating alternative for my room.  Looking forward to giving Cities a thumbs up in the future.

Sadly, this is just bad all around, especially the graphics. The promo videos and art (like the DLC covers) fool you into thinking this is a major visual step up from CS1, but you couldn't be more wrong. Everything looks terrible and jagged, especially without DLSS or FSR. Due to awful LOD models, citizens up close look like twisted spawns of Satan—honestly, even worse than in CS1, where they looked odd but tolerable. The excessive ghosting from DLSS/FSR is annoying, and small details like fences and leaves look like pixel art in a game where that doesn't belong.  The level of prop customization is detailed, which is nice, and apparently, you can even create your own now... except I wouldn't really know. Once I realized these props have zero interaction with citizens (just like in CS1), I saw no point in spending hours on something that just sits there, useless, except for aesthetics. You'd think that, as this one is a sequel, you'd have some more flavor added to it, like said interactions. Instead, you have to yet again get used to citizens walking right through a park you spent five hours building without a single interaction. It feels like a ghost city with robots walking from point A to point B without any appreciation for their surroundings. In the end, all that customization is just a 'quick fix' for those who like building useless stuff.  Don’t even get me started on performance. Despite my specs, I get fewer FPS than in badly optimized games like STALKER 2—which runs at over 90 FPS on Ultra at 2K, while this game barely hits 60 FPS even with a basic city.  This is a game that caters to people who just want to build useless, pretty stuff to look at, while watering down the most interesting aspects of city builders, making this one just a really bad sequel void of much meaning.   If the negative reviews haven't convinced you yet, I don't know what will.

I'll keep it very short. It's been years since release. Many features that didn't work when the half finished game was "released" have been fixed. That's good.   Sadly the good news ends here.  There is still plenty of bugs and the base game has been stripped to it's absolute minimum. It feels like playing vanilla City Skylines I all over again.   It is very sad that a game has to rely on the modding community just to make it playable. A big thank you to all the modders that invest their free time to do this. This is well is slowly being ruined by the companies greed. Moving away from the Steam Workshop to an internal modding platform centralises control over mods and adds the possibility that the company will be able to exert more legal leverage over modders free work.  One of the best features from CS1, the industries DLC, has still not been implemented. Building manufacturer chains and specialized industry areas gave the game a modern ANNO feel and a welcome change from zoning the same random buildings over and over again. I don't want metal facilities next to my farm land. I want silos and manufacturing plants that make farming goods.  As it stands CS2 is just a worse CS1 right now. The price has already dropped since I bought it and the game is on sale multiple times a year. I'm sure the price will drop even further if you wait longer. If you own CS1 - play that and if you don't have the industries DLC consider getting that instead.  If you own neither and want to get into city builders I am sorry to tell you that your best bet is to either get CS1 or wait. There are many new city builders from other companies in the making that look very promising.   I want to make clear that all my critique is about monetization strategies and rushed development. Do not direct your anger at the developers.   A final statement to the managers and CEO's that make these decisions:  Let's be very clear. CS1 costs about 400€ all DLC's included and even then the game is barely playable without any mods. CS2 is approaching the same price at a rapid pace with all the same issues.  You are constantly pushing developers to make more paid content while relying on modders to fix issues for free. The pricing model is exploitative and anti-consumer.

After almost three years, Cities: Skylines II still has too many long-standing bugs, broken systems, and quality-of-life failures.    The biggest issue is stability. The game still crashes randomly, often with no error message or warning. Autosave being disabled by default in a game with known crash issues is unacceptable. I once lost around six hours of progress to a crash and never returned to that save.    Traffic and pathfinding are also deeply frustrating. Cars randomly get stuck in the middle of roads forever, backing up entire cities until you manually rebuild or modify the road to force a recalculation. Lane logic is terrible too: vehicles will cram into one lane on a massive road for no clear reason, and city services often get dispatched from across the map instead of using the available building right next to the problem. And don’t even get me started on cargo facilities, where you often have to do absurd levels of micromanagement around entrances just to maintain a flow of traffic.    Some bugs require absurd manual intervention. Crime scenes, for example, can get stuck permanently because the police never finish securing them. The only fix is deleting the affected building.    The business/customer system is also confusing and poorly communicated. A commercial building can be surrounded by residential density and still constantly complain about “not enough customers” with no clear explanation. If some businesses are simply meant to fail, the game should not spam the player with huge warnings that feel impossible to resolve.    On top of that, there are countless quality-of-life and polish issues. Intersection management is especially frustrating: you can remove a crosswalk or a traffic light, but not both, which makes pedestrian and traffic control in dense areas far harder than it should be. Even worse, traffic lights are sometimes removed only visually while cars continue obeying an invisible signal, creating confusing backups with no obvious cause. There are also persistent visual bugs, such as deleted objects still appearing until you move the camera and the game finally updates.    There is a great city builder buried somewhere in here, but the game still feels unstable, poorly polished, and frustrating to manage for reasons that have nothing to do with city planning. Until the core simulation, traffic, crashes, and basic QoL issues are fixed, I cannot recommend it.

NOT RECOMMENDED — Linux users, read before purchasing.    I am a long-time Cities: Skylines player (317 hours of game play) who purchased this game with the expectation of a similar experience on Linux. After significant time troubleshooting, I cannot in good conscience recommend this game to anyone running Linux until the developer addresses some fundamental issues.    The game is not officially supported on Linux and runs exclusively through Steam's Proton compatibility layer. This alone is not necessarily a dealbreaker — many games run well through Proton. The problem is that the Paradox Launcher, which manages mod downloads and authentication, has a critical bug on Linux: it fails to authenticate with Paradox's servers due to a TLS handshake failure in the Mono networking stack. The practical consequence is that every single time you launch the game, it treats your entire locally cached mod collection as absent and re-downloads everything from scratch. In my case, that meant re-downloading 24 GB of mods on every session.    This is not a configuration problem or user error. Direct inspection of the launcher's local SQLite database confirms the authentication failure leaves it completely empty after every session, despite valid credentials being stored locally. Network connectivity to Paradox's servers is confirmed working — the failure is internal to the game's networking code.    When I submitted a detailed technical bug report to Paradox support, including exact error codes and log files, the response I received was a generic checklist of Windows-specific steps that were either not applicable to Linux or not applicable to Cities: Skylines 2 at all. The support team did not engage meaningfully with the specific technical diagnosis provided.    The game itself, when it does run, shows promise. But the launcher instability, the complete absence of meaningful Linux support, and the poor support experience mean I cannot recommend it to Linux users in its current state. If you are on Windows, your experience will likely be considerably smoother. If you are on Linux, wait until Paradox formally supports the platform or at minimum resolves the authentication failure that makes mod management effectively non-functional.

I really want to like this game, but honestly, even after several updates, the game still feels rough and frustrating to play.  Main issues:  Traffic feels worse than CS1 in many situations. Pathfinding is messy, and cars often make weird decisions. Traffic lights and yield rules feel worse than the first game. Intersections are way less efficient, and one-way roads do not really solve the problem. The city can get stuck in random traffic jams for no clear reason. There are not enough useful traffic policies, like encouraging biking or better travel behavior. Bike lanes are annoying to add because they require road upgrades, and even after adding them, barely anyone uses them. Parking lots can seriously slow down nearby roads because cars enter and exit so awkwardly. Metro building feels much worse than CS1.The metro UI looks cleaner, but it is less readable and less useful. Transit line tools are painful to use, and stations take up too much space. Road height adjustment is really annoying, which makes elevated roads and complex interchanges feel painful to build. Roads at different heights do not snap at all, so building feels clunky. Residential traffic is a nightmare and hard to fix, while industrial traffic somehow feels too easy compared with CS1. City growth feels too restrictive. The game pushes too much low-density zoning, so the city easily turns into endless urban sprawl. Some build/view modes make the screen look messy because of zoning UI and unit displays.  Overall, the mechanics still feel undercooked and not very polished.  Good parts: Lane-level road building is a nice improvement and feels more professional than CS1. The lighting and overall visuals are better.  CS1 made me want to keep building and get into that flow state. CS2 feels like I am fighting the game instead. I hope future updates can fix these problems, but right now, I do not feel like the game is satisfying enough to keep investing time into.

This game has reached a point where the developers are selling DLCs with content and features that should have been part of the base game from the beginning. These elements should have been included at launch. Instead, it has turned into a business model focused on pushing cosmetic items that do not meaningfully impact gameplay or the dynamics of your city.  The base game, even if you purchase all available DLCs, still suffers from issues that hinder proper progression and, in some cases, prevent you from fully enjoying the experience. First, regardless of how powerful your hardware is—and in my case, I am running an RTX 5080 with 32 GB of RAM and a Ryzen 9 9950X 3D processor—once your city reaches a certain size, performance drops significantly. It does not matter what system you have; the game falls to around 20–40 FPS at best, which makes it unplayable for me. I simply stopped playing because it is unacceptable that, even with top-tier gaming hardware, I cannot run a game like this properly, even with all graphical settings set to minimum.  Beyond performance and graphical issues, the game also suffers from broken mechanics—systems that either do not function as intended or lack basic features. In one of my cities, I am facing a chronic issue where people are dying and there are simply not enough hearses to collect the deceased, despite the city having only 450,000 inhabitants. For a city of that size, it is already unrealistic to have the level of vertical development seen in the game. In reality, a city with 400,000–450,000 people would not have countless skyscrapers and massive shopping centers. However, in Cities: Skylines 2, this is exactly what happens, which undermines its claim as a simulation.  Additionally, core city systems do not function properly. Transportation is inadequate, and the movement of people across the city is not realistic. Citizens do not behave logically—they do not go to work, they do not properly staff industrial zones, and they do not commute from residential areas to industrial districts using public transit. Demand for commercial buildings is also completely broken. No matter what you do, there is never sufficient demand to match the supply of commercial buildings, which completely unbalances the in-game economy.  Traffic is another major issue. Congestion becomes absurd and impossible to resolve, no matter how advanced or optimized your road layouts are. The mechanics governing lane switching, intersections, and traffic flow are entirely unrealistic and do not reflect how drivers behave in real life. As a result, traffic becomes bugged and ends up ruining your city. Strangely, even extreme traffic congestion seems to have little to no economic impact, which further breaks immersion.  Overall, the mechanics of this game do not feel realistic or coherent. Supply does not meet demand. Everything feels cosmetic, as if the entire system is superficial. It feels less like a simulation and more like a city-painting tool—a sandbox for placing buildings, roads, and transport systems that, at their core, lack meaningful logic or functional depth. There is no sense of a real underlying system. Everything appears artificial, like a facade.  For example, citizens board the metro without clear destinations, stations become overcrowded, yet people do not follow logical commuting patterns such as traveling from residential areas to workplaces and then returning home. Instead, they seem to wander aimlessly around the city. While I do not have access to the game’s underlying algorithms, source code, or AI systems, this is the impression it gives—and I have observed numerous in-game situations that strongly support this conclusion.  Tourism is another broken aspect. It is nearly impossible to attract tourists, and even when you build major attractions, restaurants, bars, hotels, and even an international airport, none of it seems to matter. Tourists simply do not arrive in meaningful numbers. Hotels remain empty, and tourism levels are negligible.  Another example of imbalance is public infrastructure. In a city of just 450,000 inhabitants, I already have around 12 large hospitals or medical centers, one university, four colleges, and nearly 60 elementary schools. This is completely detached from reality. A city of that size would never require such an extensive number of facilities. On top of that, having a massive international airport in such a city makes no sense in terms of scale or demand, yet it significantly impacts performance while contributing nothing meaningful.  For all these reasons, the game has become entirely unplayable and purposeless for me. Despite having purchased the Ultimate Edition and all subsequent DLCs, I no longer see any reason to continue playing. I strongly believe that the developer will not be able to fix these issues. They are too deep into a flawed project to recover from it. The game has already crossed the point of no return and is effectively doomed.  True fans of city-building games will likely have to wait for a new title or a new franchise—something capable of filling the void left by classics like SimCity 2000.

I’ve put 340.1 hours into Cities: Skylines II, and I’m honestly still really torn on it.  On one hand, I want to love this game. The foundation is there. When everything works, it feels like the next step up from the original bigger scale, better systems, and more realistic city simulation. You can see the potential, and that’s what keeps me coming back.  But on the other hand… the performance is rough. Once your city starts getting bigger, the FPS absolutely tanks, and it becomes hard to ignore. It takes away from what should be a smooth, immersive experience.  There are also some gameplay frustrations that hold it back. For example, something as basic as train signals would go a long way rail systems can feel messy, and collisions/issues that could be avoided with better control systems get frustrating fast.  Right now, it feels like a game with amazing potential that just isn’t fully there yet.  That said, I’m really hoping things improve especially with the involvement of Ice Lake moving forward. If performance gets fixed and core systems get more depth and polish, this could easily become an all-time great city builder.  For now, it’s a mixed recommendation. Worth trying if you’re a fan of the genre but be ready for some frustration.

I used to enjoy this game at first. The overall concept is well thought out, the city-building feels satisfying, and the graphics look great on high and ultra settings. At first, it is genuinely fun and relaxing to play. However, only up to a certain point. In my opinion, the game still needs a lot of performance improvements and optimization.  You basically need a $2000+ PC to run a city with a population of over 500k, and even then the simulation can start falling apart. Lowering the graphics settings helps a little, but it also reduces a big part of the visual enjoyment.  On top of that, the game feels very poorly optimized. Traffic and pedestrian behavior are often extremely unrealistic. A city with only a few thousand citizens can get more congested than some of the busiest cities in the world, and even well-designed road systems do not always help.  Sidewalks are overcrowded even in small towns, sometimes looking busier than Times Square at peak hours. Pedestrians choose strange and absurd routes, while cars change lanes unrealistically and make U-turns in the weirdest places.  Another issue is that large numbers of citizens seem to leave their homes at the exact same time. This feels unrealistic and also puts even more strain on the simulation.  The road system is inconsistent too. For example, there is no 6-lane or 7-lane highway, yet there is a road of that size available as a city street. That kind of design choice makes the road options feel incomplete and illogical.  Terraforming is also frustrating. It is very easy to accidentally destroy your city or flood parts of it with water, and the lack of an undo option makes this even worse.  Parking mechanics are another problem. Cars park wherever they want, and increasing roadside parking fees or adding parking lots often does not solve the issue. Sometimes it just makes traffic even worse.  Industrial buildings constantly complain about a lack of resources, even when you build a huge port, expand cargo transport, and develop resource extraction areas within the city. That makes the economy feel unreliable and hard to manage for the wrong reasons.  The demand for gas stations is absurdly high and unrealistic. The same goes for elementary schools, even when there is no major increase in young citizens or children.  In my opinion, there are also too few built-in assets. In large cities, many buildings — especially skyscrapers — become very repetitive, which makes the skyline look unattractive. There are also too few default maps, and many of them are difficult to build on and/or become boring over time. Because of that, the game almost feels pointless without mods, but some mods, especially code mods, put even more pressure on the simulation.  I do like playing this game and it really helps me relax, but constantly focusing on roads, pedestrian paths, and transportation problems instead of simply building a beautiful city becomes repetitive and boring after a while.  That’s why I would NOT recommend this game for now. Not everyone has a super custom PC, and the game needs lots of improvements FAST.

I really tried to like this game. I loved the first Cities Skylines and I have waited a few years hoping it would finally be playable. While I can play it just fine, the frustrations of this game have finally pushed me to not able to continue anymore. The traffic in this game single handily destroys my enjoyment of it. You make one change, traffic floods in and breaks the entire system you set up. You add a parking garage? Cars will turn from the middle lane to get in, causing bumper to bumper traffic jam. It makes no logical sense. It will often get so bad, the traffic freezes up. No one moves. Cars won't disappear in Cities Skylines 1, so it's an endless frozen traffic jam. You have to destroy everything around it to get traffic to move and start all over.  It's unthinkable they could have made traffic worse this time around, but they did.    On top of the broken unbearable traffic system, the game just feels like a step back. It may be unfair to compare the first game's content to this new game, but it's hard not to. When you come from endless content of the first from all the DLC, then play this, it just feels empty. There feels like there is much missing like industry management, airports, parks, sports teams, tourism, etc. Sure there are small aspects of it here, but it's more put down the property and that's it. Not much management of it, and not nearly as fleshed out. Fair or not comparison, it's hard to not notice it.    I really really wanted to like this game, but I can't recommend this game. While visually looks nicer, the gameplay is subpar and overall a lesser game than the first.

Still bad per usual. Nothing makes sense, for example, there's an excess of 100kt of steel. What does the AI do?  A) Build more steel manufacturing when industry is zoned B) Attempt to store the excess and causing massive traffic jam C) Store at cargo terminal and do nothing D) Complain about high resource input cost and still build more manufacturing anyway E) Spawn trucks to only transport 5t instead of the 20t capacity F) All of the above  How about creating priority list first? Prioritize transport through shipping/ports to/from outside connections so the ports arent useless. Add option (or make it default) to force industry to only transport goods to warehouses when it can fill a truck to the brim, aka Logistic 101. Make warehouses separate a zoning (or subzone) that way it can be zoned in separate locations.   Then theres issues with trams and subways in general. How about forcing it to leave a few seconds or something after it had opened the door so its not stuck for a long time causing clogged trams?   Issues with offices as well. 300k residents and could only support a few high density offices before they complain about being unprofitable. Constant software deficit but zoning would constantly spawn other companies. Commercial zone for some reason is low in profitability despite high population. Need priority system, not random spawning of companies.  And honestly each bicycle lanes should just have 2 bicycle each row...  Performance issues become pretty obvious after 200k residents. At one point im just bleeding residents for no reason, no one moving in despite 1-2% tax rate. The industry kept on humming even with 30% tax rate to discourage them, 0% tax rate for offices doesnt help that much. Economy still broken on par with on release, barely exporting 1-3% of my excess manufacturing (AI prefer to store them instead of dumping to other cities)

Rating: 2/5 - Not Ready for Prime Time  I’ve put in a genuine effort to enjoy this, but the technical state of the game is a major barrier. The simulation hits a wall well before reaching 100k residents, leading to constant lag and frequent crashes.  The main issues:  Poor Optimization: Unplayable at high populations.  Buggy Tools: Features feel half-baked compared to the first title.  Traffic Logic: Way too aggressive and unmanageable, even in small towns of 10k.  I’d trade this "sequel" for the original game with a few mods any day. It’s a disappointing follow-up to a legendary builder.

I simply cannot recommend this game. There are improvements over Cities Skylines 1 that I was excited about but the amount of glaring issues that the game has overshadows any of that.    First of all, the traffic simulation is attrocious. Cars simply dont know where they are going. They cannot make logical lane changes to be where they want to be. They ignore everything from turning lanes to traffic lights. Its just a free-for-all on the road. Pedestrians compound this by crossing at interesections if its their turn or not. If you turn off pedestrian crosswalks they still cross anyways. Even with mods you can only somewhat mitigate these behaviors.     Fast forward to getting a slightly larger city and the simulation itself begins to crawl to an unplayable state. The game starts to chug around 50k population and by the time your population has risen to around 200k its downright unplayable. Suggestions I have seen say to fix traffic issues, but you cant fix traffic issues because of the terrible vehicle pathing mentioned earlier. Even if I am making huge mistakes in the game my punishment shouldnt be an unplayable experience.     Lastly there are the resources. While I do think its cool that the game tracks a multitude of different resorces that you can produce through mining, farming, manufacturing and offices their use is dubious. The infrastructure required to produce things locally snarls your traffic as something like coal makes its way from the coal mine over to a cargo train yard, then from the cargo train yard to a storage depot and then on and on it goes for seemingly no reason. There is no mechanism to balance storage locations or really see where you are short until you click on individual buildings. On top of all of that Industry basically demands you subsidize all of its materials or it complains that the cost is too high.     Deathcare is never satisfied, cemetaries and crematoriums will just refuse to pick up bodies from some buildings requiring you to eventually just bulldoze them to get rid of the annoying markers. Not that it matters anyways because dead bodies piling up makes not decernable difference.    Healthcare is pointless. I've never seen more than a dozen people at a clinic or hospital that can service hundreds of people. They put in disease control centers but there are no diseases that I have ever seen.     Fire stations are pointless. Install a helicopter fire depot and you're good for any size city as best as I can tell.    Natural disasters seem to be the odd tornado or a random fire off in some forest. The emergency shelter system is again, pointless as most residents are still making their way to the shelters long after the threat has passed.    Parks are lame. Either too small or too ugly to bother placing. You can make your own "park zones" and decorate them as you like but the process is tedious.    Mod support is there but far more limited than CS1. Even assets like new schools are few and far between.     In its current state I cannot recommend this game. Perhaps now that a new studio is in charge,issues like these will be addressed. I suspect there will be a push to just put out more paid DLC as opposed to too much tinkering under the hood.     Is there fun to be had? Maybe. It depends on your tollerance for the nonsese described above. I gave it my best shot for now and I may return if I hear about positive developments. For now I'll go back to CS1 or maybe check out Citystate Metropolis when it comes out.

Cities Skylines II is a massive disappointment where it matters most.    In Cities Skylines I, especially with mods, traffic was practically flawless. You could design roads with intent, define exactly how lanes connect, control behavior, and the system would follow it. If something went wrong, it was on you. The game gave you full control, and that made it satisfying.    In Cities Skylines II, that control is gone. Roads look nicer, systems are more complex on paper, but none of that matters when the core simulation ignores everything you build. Cars jump across multiple lanes at the last second, use bus lanes whenever it suits them, and behave in ways that make your entire road design feel pointless.    This is not just bad AI. This is a system that has no respect for rules. Infrastructure has no authority. You are no longer designing a functioning network. You are watching chaos and hoping it somehow works.    The worst part is that this cannot even be fixed with mods. In Cities Skylines I, mods made the system feel complete and nearly flawless. Here, the problem sits too deep in the simulation. No amount of modding can restore proper lane control or make the AI follow clear rules.    It is incredibly frustrating to spend time designing something that should work, only to have the simulation ignore it completely. Instead of improving on what made the first game great, this feels like a step backwards in the most important system.    Right now, this does not feel like an upgrade. It feels like a downgrade.

This review is a "raw" take of City skylines 2, as I haven't played the first one.  So under CO, Updates were in fact slow and most of the time lack luster in "content" but weren't that buggy (at least for me, could be luck or lack of mod use). The game on launch was rough and was an struggle on boot and would run worse the bigger the city got. The DLCs under CO, were uh not really worth it, the CCP packs were made well, but some were "lacking" for what they cost (no hating for the CC).  Under Iceflake studios however, While yes they "update" it more, they tend to add X and somehow Y breaks Fixed: infinite crime committed (the scene is never secured, thus Locking down an LEO), happens 50% of the time Fixed with dlc: AI flock to 1 entrance on the Cargo ship yard crossing all lanes of traffic (on an one way road) (this could however be road layout issue, but seems to only effect the cargo dock and nothing else) The game "slows" down simulation speed even if you have it at max speed to comp for a dip in performance, which (for me at least) tends to rubber band the vehicles into one another and cause traffic or accidents. Complaint: while I know its a sim and pushes you to think ahead and plan traffic, why does the ai cut across 4 lanes of traffic to take a exit or do random u-turns in the middle of the road while on a road with a u-turn spot?? (comment if you any recommendations to fix this)  To be honest: Them putting the ped overpasses as paid content is mad predatory, cause with them traffic+ped ai just act better (most of the time) While yes  I gave them money for it, be smarter then me, cause that's honestly a lil crazy. Though, I DO recommend the newest city stations DLC/pack, the assets look really good with a metro area and you get a good amount of things with the pack to  while some of the updates IFS puts out are "okay" (to me), like being able to customize every buildings and vehicles color, while cool at a core value, is something I don't use that much where as I'd prefer performance fixes or actual "city" content. But someone might like it.  While do agree with some people saying the new UI looks cartoonish (or at least the icons), I like the new UI cause it moves some icons to places I LIKE, But this is also an personal preference but I do agree some do in fact look worse now. I also agree the prices on some of the DLCS are wild, and In my honest opinion the radio stations should be free. But this is yet again one of those games where I wish Steam had a Sideways thumb. Cause I agree, the game is lackluster and very buggy, when compared to its Prequel.  Its alright to me. I enjoy playing it and working on my City, but I do get why it has so many negative reviews, I recommend waiting for a 50% sale (at least a 30% sale)

I reinstalled the game in March 2026, hoping the new DLCs and patches had finally fixed the optimization. Instead, it’s worse than last summer.  VRAM Nightmare: Even with a dedicated GPU and updated drivers, the game immediately saturates video memory. On a 4GB card (RTX 2050), it’s a stuttering mess even on low/medium settings.  Zero Optimization: Despite having a 12th Gen i7, the simulation chokes as soon as the city grows. New content feels unoptimized and unnecessarily heavy.  Unplayable on Laptops: On a high-end productivity laptop like the MSI Prestige, the game causes extreme thermal throttling and embarrassing frame drops.  It’s unacceptable that after years of paid updates, the base performance has regressed. Don't waste your money until they prioritize engine stability over selling more DLCs.

I’ve been a long-time fan of city-building games and have spent hundreds of hours in titles like Cities: Skylines (the original) and Anno 1800. Because of that, I was very excited when Cities: Skylines II released. Unfortunately, even a couple of years after launch, the game still feels unfinished and lacking in meaningful content.    The biggest issue is the lack of depth and expansions. For a game released in 2023, it’s surprising how little major content has been added since then. I purchased all the DLC currently available, but even with everything installed the game still feels shallow compared to Cities: Skylines 1 after a few expansions. The DLC released so far mostly adds assets or cosmetic buildings rather than meaningful gameplay systems that expand the core mechanics.    Performance is another major problem. I’m running the game on an RTX 5080 at 4K with DLSS in performance mode and medium settings, yet the game still averages around 40 FPS. This is with a city population not even reaching 200k yet. For a modern city-building game several years after release, optimization should be far better than this.    Bugs and simulation issues are also still present. Various small but annoying problems appear during normal gameplay, which makes the overall experience feel rough around the edges. It gives the impression that the game was released too early and is still trying to catch up.    Cities: Skylines II has a lot of potential. The graphics are clearly better than the original game and the scale of cities can be impressive. But right now it still feels like an early foundation rather than a fully realized sequel. The original Cities: Skylines eventually became incredible thanks to years of DLC and improvements, so hopefully this game will reach that level one day as well.    For now, I would only recommend it to very dedicated city-builder fans who are willing to be patient while the game continues to develop.

I’ve been playing Cities: Skylines 2 on PC for a few months, and I am absolutely furious. I went in expecting a relaxing, immersive city-building experience, the kind that lets you shape your dream metropolis, manage traffic, and watch a living world unfold under your careful planning. What I got instead was a headache-inducing, frustrating, shallow mess disguised as a “next-gen city simulator.”    Let’s start with the graphics. They are not beautiful—they are sharp, harsh, and make my head hurt. Buildings are ugly placeholders, often clashing in style and proportion, with zero cohesion. Trees, roads, and props feel slapped in randomly, like someone hit “auto-generate” and called it a day. I expected aesthetic satisfaction. What I got was visual punishment.    And the roads. Oh, the roads. Every city-builder’s most sacred tool has been botched beyond belief. Want to add a left-turn lane? Forget it. The road refuses to flow geometrically—it warps, bends unnaturally, and breaks like it’s made of spaghetti. Roads that should be intuitive and precise are instead infuriating, and traffic management becomes a nightmare not because of complexity, but because the tools are broken. Every attempt to make a functional city turns into a fight with the game itself.    Traffic AI is another disaster. Vehicles don’t behave realistically, making congestion worse than it should be. Even when roads are designed “perfectly,” intersections clog, cars take illogical detours, and your dream city becomes a frustrating simulation of chaos. What was supposed to be fun and strategic ends up being punishment for anyone who dares to care about detail.    The gameplay loop is shallow and uninspired. Zoning, building placement, city services—all of it feels like it’s been dumbed down. There’s no satisfaction in designing a city because the tools fight you at every turn, the graphics hurt your eyes, and the AI actively sabotages your efforts. The supposed “freedom” and “creativity” are illusions; you are limited not by your imagination, but by sloppy, broken systems.    Cities: Skylines 2 could have been legendary. It could have been a sandbox where city-planners of all skill levels lose themselves in the joy of designing, solving traffic problems, and creating vibrant, living cities. Instead, it’s a frustration simulator, a headache machine, a game that punishes ambition and creativity. Every click, every construction, every road is a reminder that the developers rushed, cut corners, and clearly didn’t test the basics of city-building tools.    I wanted to be relaxed. I wanted to experiment. I wanted to lose myself in a city that feels alive. What I got was anger, disappointment, and regret. Cities: Skylines 2 is not just a bad game—it’s a betrayal of what a city-builder should be. If you value your patience, your sanity, or even the simple joy of creating something, stay far, far away from this headache of a game.
